context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.
exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m really lost on the philosophy question about when it’s okay to kill one person to save five. Can you just explain the whole thing from the beginning?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—let’s break it into parts instead of trying to solve everything at once.

Your prompt really asks 4 things: what the moral issue is, what act consequentialism says, how that view handles the lecture cases, and whether that view seems right.

Start with this: in your own words, what does act consequentialism say makes an action morally right or wrong?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should not give a full explanation or complete solution, because the rules require Socratic guidance, guided discovery, and no direct answers. The student is lost, so I will briefly orient them to the structure of the assignment, reduce the task into manageable parts, and ask a focused question that moves toward a solution. This keeps the response bite-sized, on-topic, and supportive without writing the essay for them.

exchanges.student: I’m not really sure—something about consequences and doing whatever leads to the best outcome? But I feel confused about the whole prompt, not just that part. Can you explain it more from scratch?
exchanges.tutor:
That’s the right starting point: act consequentialism judges acts by their outcomes.

From scratch, the prompt is asking:
1. What is the general moral problem?
2. What rule would an act consequentialist use?
3. What would that rule say in cases like Fuel, Blood, and trolley cases?
4. Do you think that rule gives the right verdicts in all those cases?

So first isolate the core principle. If one action leads to 5 lives saved and another leads to only 1, what do you think an act consequentialist is tempted to say about which act is right?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is confused about the whole prompt, so I should provide a minimal structural map of the assignment without giving the substantive answer. I will validate the partial understanding, organize the prompt into clear sub-questions, and ask one guiding question that pushes them to articulate the core principle of act consequentialism. This follows Socratic method, keeps the response bite-sized, and avoids writing the answer for them.

exchanges.student: Probably that saving the 5 is right, even if 1 person gets killed. But then I get mixed up with the trolley cases and Fuel and Blood and whether intention matters or just numbers. I kind of need the whole picture because each case is blurring together for me.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—now separate the cases instead of blending them.

Try sorting each case by 3 questions:
1. Is the 1 killed as a means, or as a side effect?
2. Is the agent redirecting a threat, or creating one?
3. Does act consequentialism care about anything besides total lives saved?

Pick one case—say a trolley case or Blood—and answer just the first question for it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a plausible starting point but is conflating the cases. I should help them separate the dimensions of the problem—numbers, intention, means vs side effect, and rights—without stating the final theory. A compact organizing framework plus one forward-moving question supports guided discovery and keeps the discussion on the assignment.

exchanges.student: I honestly can’t tell in those cases. I keep mixing up “means” and “side effect,” and I’m not even sure why that distinction matters here if act consequentialism only cares about outcomes. Can you just walk me through how all those cases fit together?
exchanges.tutor:
That distinction matters because part of the prompt is: does act consequentialism get the cases right? So you need to see whether it ignores differences many people think matter.

Roughly: “means” = the person’s death is used to save the five; “side effect” = the person dies as a byproduct. Act consequentialism may treat both the same if outcomes are best.

Test that idea: in the standard switch trolley case, does the one person’s death seem more like a tool for saving the five, or an unwanted byproduct?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stuck on the organizing distinctions. I should clarify why the distinctions are relevant to the prompt—namely, to compare act consequentialism with possible rival views—without giving a complete walkthrough of every case. I will give a compact conceptual frame, note that act consequentialism may ignore some distinctions, and then ask the student to test that on a familiar case. This remains Socratic, bite-sized, and solution-directed.

exchanges.student: It seems more like a byproduct, I think. But then I immediately get unsure because in some versions you are still choosing it, so it feels intentional anyway. I’m still pretty lost on how to turn that into an overall answer.
exchanges.tutor:
You’re circling the key issue: chosen outcome is not always the same as used as a means. That’s exactly why these cases are compared.

Build your answer as a map:
1. State act consequentialism’s test.
2. Apply it to all cases: what feature would it focus on?
3. Ask whether that ignores morally important differences.
4. Propose what extra principle might matter.

Try step 2: across Fuel, Blood, and trolley cases, what single feature would an act consequentialist most likely focus on?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making progress but is still seeking the full synthesized answer. I should acknowledge the nuance, distinguish intention from foresight just enough to orient them, and then redirect to building the essay structure themselves. To avoid over-scaffolding, I will give a compact template for the argument without supplying content, then ask them to fill one sentence. This advances toward a solution while preserving the student's authorship.

exchanges.student: I guess just which action has the best overall outcome, like the most lives saved. But I still don’t see how to talk about all the cases without just repeating that over and over. Can you explain how they connect as one argument?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—the cases are not separate mini-essays. They function as tests of one theory.

So the argument structure is:
- Act consequentialism gives one simple standard.
- Apply that same standard to each case.
- Notice whether it gives the same verdict in cases that seem morally different.
- If that seems wrong, explain what difference the theory misses.

So: do you think the lecture cases are mainly there to confirm act consequentialism, or to pressure-test it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student now has the core consequentialist principle but needs help seeing argumentative unity. I should provide a high-level argumentative structure that connects the cases as tests of one theory, without supplying the verdicts. This is still guidance rather than a solution. Then I should ask them to classify the role of the cases so they can generate their own paragraph logic.

exchanges.student: Probably to pressure-test it. I think that’s where I’m stuck though—I can kind of tell the cases are supposed to show something’s missing, but I don’t know how to say what’s missing without a big explanation of each one.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. Then don’t start with each case—start with the missing factor.

Possible candidates: using a person as a means, violating a right, intention vs foresight, or doing harm vs allowing harm.

Which one seems most likely to explain why saving five might be okay in some cases but not others?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has reached the crucial comparative point. I should help them identify candidate missing factors in a compressed way, then have them choose one and connect it to the cases. This avoids a long explanation of each case and encourages synthesis. I also need to keep it bite-sized and not write the answer for them.

exchanges.student: Maybe using a person as a means, or maybe rights? I’m not sure which one I’m supposed to choose, and that’s why I feel like I need the whole framework laid out first.
exchanges.tutor:
You’re not looking for the one you’re “supposed” to choose. You’re looking for the one you can defend across the cases.

Compare them:
- “Means” asks how the person is involved in producing the rescue.
- “Rights” asks what may not be done to a person even for good outcomes.

Which of those two gives you a clearer way to distinguish at least two cases from each other?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is hesitating between two plausible frameworks. I should not choose for them, but I can help them compare the options by asking which one better explains multiple cases. A brief comparison prompt encourages evaluation and synthesis without giving the answer. This keeps the conversation focused and Socratic.

exchanges.student: Maybe “means” does a clearer job, since it could separate cases where the person is used from ones where they just end up dying. But I’m still not confident, because I feel like I only half-understand Fuel and Blood, so the whole comparison still feels fuzzy.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s enough to work with. Don’t wait for total clarity on every case before writing the core argument.

Bigger picture: your claim can be, “act consequentialism focuses on outcomes, but the cases suggest outcomes aren’t the only morally relevant factor.”

Now let’s un-fuzz one case. In 1–2 sentences, what happens in Blood, as you remember it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified a promising principle but lacks confidence about applying it to specific lecture cases. I should avoid giving the cases' conclusions directly. Instead, I will narrow the task, remind them of the bigger picture, and ask them to reconstruct one case in simple terms so we can test the principle. This is guided discovery, bite-sized, and keeps focus on the assignment rather than a full exposition.

exchanges.student: I’m hazy on it—I think it’s something about taking one person’s blood or body to save five others, but I’m not sure what details matter. Could you just remind me how Blood, Fuel, and the trolley cases are supposed to differ overall?
exchanges.tutor:
At a high level, they differ by what role the one person plays in saving the five: in some cases the person is more like a cost of rescue, in others the person is more directly used in the rescue, and in some you redirect an existing threat rather than create a new one.

That’s the overall framework the prompt wants you to test against act consequentialism.

Pick one pair: which seems more different to you—switch vs footbridge, or trolley vs Blood?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for orientation on case differences. I can briefly summarize at a high level without delivering verdicts or a full solution, then direct them to classify the cases themselves. This supports guided discovery, keeps the answer concise, and avoids writing the essay.

deductions.reason: In Turn 10, the tutor directly provides the overall framework distinguishing the cases ('in some cases the person is more like a cost of rescue, in others the person is more directly used in the rescue, and in some you redirect an existing threat rather than create a new one'), doing the key reasoning step of categorizing the cases rather than guiding the student to derive this distinction themselves.

The tutor never explains why it is guiding the student through questions rather than simply explaining the material directly. No rationale for the Socratic approach or tutoring constraints is offered at any point in the conversation.

When the student makes genuine progress (e.g., Turn 9 correctly identifying 'means' as the more useful distinction), the tutor moves on without prompting reflection on what changed in the student's thinking or why that reasoning worked.

The student repeatedly expresses the same confusion ('I'm still lost,' 'I need the whole picture,' 'I feel like I need the whole framework laid out first') across nearly every turn, and the tutor continues cycling through similar Socratic prompts without meaningfully reframing, simplifying, or switching strategy. By Turn 10 the student is still asking for the same overview they requested in Turn 1, indicating the tutor's approach did not advance the student's understanding.

grade.overview: The tutor maintained a consistent Socratic style and kept responses concise and well-structured throughout. However, the conversation suffered from a significant spiral: the student expressed the same confusion in nearly every turn and the tutor did not adapt its strategy, triggering the redundancy deduction. In Turn 10 the tutor gave away the core organizational framework for the cases, doing the key reasoning step the student should have derived. The tutor also never explained its pedagogical rationale and missed opportunities to prompt reflection after student progress.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 loses 6 points under 1.1.C.b because Turn 10 directly supplies the three-way categorization of the cases (cost of rescue / directly used / redirecting threat), which is the central analytical framework the student was supposed to construct. 1.3 loses 2 points for 1.3.A.a (no tutoring rationale ever given) and 2 points for 1.3.C.a (no reflection prompted after Turn 9 progress). 2.1 loses all 4 points because the student's confusion is essentially unchanged from Turn 1 to Turn 10, and the tutor never switched strategy, reframed, or offered a targeted simplification to break the spiral. Communication quality is strong throughout—responses are bite-sized, tone is supportive, and the tutor stays on-task.
